Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd mostly just show up to everything in person because that's where I actually learn, but sitting down to do coursework at home was another story. I'd maybe do a bit here and there when I felt like it, but nothing structured. Being a kinesthetic learner, I need to be doing something hands-on, so just reading or watching videos at home felt pointless to me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go weeks barely touching anything, then maybe cram a bit if something was due. Deadlines didn't even motivate me that much because I knew I could pick things up in class anyway. I showed up to nearly every session, so I figured I was absorbing enough just by being there and participating.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got a notification or when someone in my peer group mentioned something was posted. Sometimes I'd check just to see if there were any announcements I'd missed. It wasn't really a habit, more like a reaction to external prompts. If nobody mentioned anything, I could go days without thinking about it.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a rhythm with the LMS. I'd log in, grab what I needed, and then leave. The peer support group helped a bit because they'd remind me about stuff, but even then it felt more like checking a box than actually engaging with the platform regularly.
Q5: The video content was decent when I actually watched it, which is why I have those minutes logged. I'd sometimes put them on while doing other things, letting the information sink in passively. Discussion posts were occasionally useful when my peers explained things in simpler terms than the lectures did. But honestly, nothing on the LMS felt as useful as just being in the room and doing things hands-on.
Q6: Most of the readings and supplementary materials I ignored completely. They just didn't work for how I learn. I'd open them, skim for maybe thirty seconds, and then close them. The assignments I barely touched because they felt disconnected from what I was actually learning in person. I know my completion rate was terrible, but I just couldn't bring myself to care about busywork.
Q7: If I could see an immediate, practical application, I'd engage with it. Like if an activity involved actually doing something or building something, I was in. But if it was just reading theory or answering questions that felt abstract, I'd check out mentally. I needed to feel like it mattered right now, not just for some future exam.
Q8: Required activities felt like obligations I resented, while optional ones I just ignored entirely. At least with required stuff there was some external pressure, even if I still didn't complete most of it. Optional activities? I couldn't even pretend to care. My motivation was already low, so adding more to my plate without consequences meant it wasn't happening.
Q9: Yeah, absolutely. I'd download slides or PDFs once and then work from those offline, usually on my phone or printed out. I'd also just rely heavily on what I learned in class, taking mental notes and then processing it later by talking it through with friends. A lot of my actual studying happened away from any screen.
Q10: Most of my learning happened in class through participation and observation. I'd watch demonstrations, try things myself, ask questions in the moment. None of that shows up anywhere in the LMS. Also, I'd study with my peer group in person, working through problems together, and that was way more effective than anything online.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides or a key reading once, then never open it on the LMS again. I'd have it saved on my laptop or phone and refer back to it when needed. So my LMS activity looked dead, but I was still using the materials, just not in a way that got tracked.
Q12: There were definitely low points, usually when assignments piled up and I felt overwhelmed by how behind I was. The anxiety would creep in, but weirdly my stress stayed low because I'd just... disengage. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, which never really happened. The peer support helped me not spiral, but it didn't exactly motivate me to do the work either.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. I work with limited resources, so I'm often tired from other responsibilities. When I'd finally have time to study, the last thing I wanted to do was sit at a computer clicking through modules. Competing responsibilities definitely won out most of the time. The LMS just became another thing I'd deal with later.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd open videos and let them play while I was doing something else entirely. Or I'd click through pages just to make it look like I was engaged, but my brain was completely elsewhere. It was going through the motions without any real absorption happening.
Q15: Constantly. I'd know an assignment was important and still put it off because the thought of starting felt overwhelming. It's not that I didn't understand the material—I actually did pretty well on exams. I just couldn't translate that into completing assignments. The disconnect between knowing and doing was frustrating, but I'd just avoid thinking about it.
Q16: Deadlines would sometimes create a small spike in my activity, but honestly not by much. I'd maybe log in the day before to see what was actually required, then decide if it was worth attempting. Most of the time I'd already accepted I wasn't going to complete things, so deadlines became more like reminders of what I was choosing to skip.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. By the time I got feedback, I'd already moved on mentally. It's not that I didn't appreciate it, but it didn't create any urgency for me to engage more. My resilience is pretty high, so negative feedback didn't crush me, but it also didn't motivate me to change course.
Q18: The course structure was fine for people who like online learning, but it didn't suit me at all. Having so much content delivered through the LMS meant I was always fighting against my natural learning style. The in-person components were great, and that's where I thrived. But the online stuff felt like an obstacle course I kept avoiding.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video content and online assignments made engagement harder for me. I learn by doing, not by watching. If there had been more hands-on components or practical tasks that I could complete in person, I would've been way more engaged. The discussion forums were okay because at least there was some interaction, but it still felt removed.
Q20: I think that's me, honestly. I'd log in, click around, watch some videos, but then never submit the actual work. It's like I was present but not productive. Maybe it's anxiety about the assignments themselves, or just a disconnect between consuming content and producing something. The LMS makes it easy to look busy without accomplishing anything.
Q21: That's exactly my situation. My exam scores were solid because I learned the material in class and through hands-on experience. The LMS activity doesn't reflect what I actually know. I'm proof that low online engagement doesn't mean low understanding. I just learned differently than the platform was designed to track.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on something and for how long, but they can't show if you actually understood it or if you were even paying attention. They miss all the offline learning, the peer discussions, the in-class participation. They're measuring presence, not learning. For someone like me, the logs make me look way worse than I actually am.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting effort into understanding it. For me, that happens in person, through doing things and discussing with others. It's not about how many times I logged in or how many videos I watched. It's about whether the material actually stuck and whether I can apply it.
Q24: The LMS completely misses my in-person engagement, which was nearly perfect. It doesn't capture the conversations I had with peers, the questions I asked in class, or the hands-on practice I did. It also doesn't show that despite my low motivation for online work, I was resilient enough to still perform well when it mattered. The data tells a story of a disengaged student, but the full picture is more complicated than that.
